title: Getting Started
description: Scaffold a Howl project with howl-init and run it locally

blocks.text: Howl is Deno-native — no Node.js, no npm, no global CLI. Deno fetches every dependency on demand.
Scaffold with @hushkey/howl-init — it runs straight from JSR. No install, no clone.
deno run -Ar jsr:@hushkey/howl-init
No fixed templates — the scaffolder composes your project from a few choices. Every project ships working dev/build/start/compile tasks, a pinned import map, and a generated typed HTTP client.

rows: The target folder and deno.json identity.
React: shadcn/ui, daisyUI, or plain Tailwind. Vue: daisyUI or plain Tailwind. shadcn vendors cn + Button + Card into client/components/ui/.
none · sqlite · postgres · mongo. Any database wires @hushkey/service-core, a sample items service, and the Studio admin at /studio.

blocks.text: Or skip the prompts with flags:

deno run -Ar jsr:@hushkey/howl-init my-app \
  --type fullstack-react \
  --ui shadcn \
  --service sqlite
The scaffolder pins the published @hushkey/howl version into your import map, so a fresh project always lines up with the docs you are reading.
Once the scaffolder finishes, jump in and start the dev server.

cd my-app
deno task dev
dev.ts boots a HowlBuilder: esbuild for pages and CSS, crawls apis/, generates the HTTP client, watches the file system, and serves on http://localhost:8000. Edit a file and the browser live-reloads.
The file system is the router. Fullstack projects split server-side code under server/ and pages under client/:

my-app/
  server/
    apis/
      public/
        ping.api.ts        ← GET /api/public/ping
    services/             ← present when you pick a database
      items/
        items.schema.ts
        items.service.ts
      connections.ts
    main.ts               ← Howl app: engine, middleware, routes
  client/
    pages/
      _app.tsx            ← HTML shell (head, scripts)
      _layout.tsx         ← shared layout for nested routes
      index.tsx           ← /
  static/                 ← served at /static/* (and root)
  generated/
    http-client.ts        ← typed client generated from apis/
  howl.config.ts          ← shared State, roles, defineApi
  dev.ts                  ← dev/build entry (HowlBuilder)
  deno.json               ← imports, tasks, compilerOptions
  AGENTS.md               ← agent guide: conventions + patterns
  CLAUDE.md               ← points Claude Code at AGENTS.md

Backend-only apps drop the client/ folder entirely. You can also run single-folder apps — put apis/, pages/, and main.ts at the project root and point HowlBuilder at them. The framework infers the layout from how you wire serverEntry / clientEntry. Every project also ships an AGENTS.md (plus a one-line CLAUDE.md pointing at it) — a per-spec guide so AI coding agents pick up the conventions immediately.

sections.heading: Adding Howl to an existing Deno project

blocks.text: Already have a Deno project? Add Howl from JSR. (For greenfield apps, prefer the scaffolder.)

deno add jsr:@hushkey/howl
# pick an engine for fullstack apps:
deno add jsr:@hushkey/howl-react   # or jsr:@hushkey/howl-vue
Wire your entry. The core has no built-in renderer — a fullstack app registers an engine explicitly:

For the React engine, set compilerOptions.jsx to "react-jsx" and jsxImportSource to "react" in deno.json. For Vue, jsxImportSource is "vue". The scaffolder writes the correct compilerOptions for the engine you pick.
The build task runs HowlBuilder in build mode, bundling a single entry under dist/.

deno task build
deno task start
The compile task produces a self-contained binary via deno compile — for bare metal or a minimal Docker image, no Deno install needed:

deno task compile
./dist/bin/my-app

See the Deployment & Performance page for the full production story, including how the Vue engine precompiles every page to an importable SSR module so deno compile binaries need no .vue source on disk.

items: Configuration — howl.config.ts, the Howl app options, and the HowlBuilder pipeline
Routing — file-system pages, layouts, dynamic params, AOT and SSG
Pages & Engines — rendering with Vue or React, page props, head, and stores
API Routes — typed endpoints with Zod validation and OpenAPI generation
Services & Studio — the document-store service layer and the admin UI
